title: Mathematicians still don’t know the fastest way to multiply numbers
excerpt: A 23-year-old student overturned an ancient conjecture about one of math’s simplest operations
content_html:
Elementary school students might memorize their times tables for single-digit numbers, but memorization won’t cut it when the teacher asks for three-digit multiplication. This requires an algorithm: students are taught to stack one number atop another and multiply each digit of the bottom number by each digit of the top one. For millennia, mathematicians believed this to be the fastest multiplication method, until a 23-year-old made a shocking discovery in 1960, which led to a mystery that remains unsolved to this day.

This mystery is critical to anyone who partakes in the digital world because multiplication is a foundational operation for computers. Encryption, robotics, artificial intelligence, audio processing and pretty much everything else we task silicon chips with involves multiplication, sometimes of huge numbers many times over. At this scale, even a simple operation becomes a bottleneck, and any extra efficiency has global economic consequences.

To understand the nature of that bottleneck, observe how the grade-school algorithm handles growth. When you multiply two two-digit numbers, you perform four single-digit multiplications. If you bump that up to a three-digit pair of numbers, you do nine single-digit multiplications. The workload scales with the square of the number of digits (n2, where n is the number of digits in the numbers being multiplied). When analyzing an algorithm like this, computer scientists don’t measure speed in seconds, because that depends on the hardware. Instead they count the computational steps. They also ignore minor bookkeeping details, such as the time it takes to carry a one when multiplying. When numbers get large enough, those lower-level operations cease to matter, entirely eclipsed by the more intensive operations involved. Computer scientists denote the number of steps using what’s called Big O notation: the grade-school algorithm, for example, takes O(n2) steps, which is read as “order n squared.” Broadly speaking, if the numbers are twice as long, the algorithm takes four times as much computational work to execute. If the numbers are a thousand times as long, it takes a million (1,000 squared) times as much work.

On supporting science journalism

If you're enjoying this article, consider supporting our award-winning journalism by subscribing. By purchasing a subscription you are helping to ensure the future of impactful stories about the discoveries and ideas shaping our world today.

Since antiquity, mathematicians have suspected that O(n2) was an inherent speed limit for multiplication. The celebrated Soviet math professor Andrey Kolmogorov posed the O(n^2) speed limit as a formal conjecture and mentioned it during a 1960 seminar at Moscow State University. Whenever mathematicians propose a conjecture, they are planting a flag of sorts and waiting for others to either prove or disprove them. It took just a week for Anatoly Karatsuba, then a 23-year-old student in the audience, to return and prove Kolmogorov wrong. Kolmogorov was stunned. The result was published in the prestigious Proceedings of the USSR Academy of Sciences, but amusingly, Karatsuba didn’t write it. Kolmogorov wrote the formal proof himself and submitted it for publication with Karatsuba listed as the lead author. Karatsuba only found out about the paper when he received the reprints in the mail.

Karatsuba’s genius was realizing that you can trade expensive, time-consuming multiplications for cheap, fast additions. Adding two n-digit numbers takes only O(n) time because it entails a single sweep through the digits rather than a complete sweep through the top number for every digit of the bottom number, as in multiplication. To see how Karatsuba traded multiplication for addition, let’s look at a small example. The method would be overly complicated for such a simple problem, but it saves a meaningful amount of time when numbers get larger.

In this simple example, let’s calculate 12 × 34.

First, we split both numbers into their tens and ones digits. Assign a = 1 and b = 2 (for 12), and c = 3 and d = 4 (for 34). Algebraically, we can rewrite 12 × 34 as (10a + b) × (10c + d).

Expanding this gives 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd).

To solve the equation in the traditional way, one must perform four distinct multiplications: ac = 3, ad = 4, bc = 6 and bd = 8, which is exactly what the grade-school stacking method entails. (Note that we don’t count the multiplications by 100 or by 10 because those only involve plopping zeroes at the ends of numbers.) Karatsuba noticed a brilliant algebraic trick. Once you compute the first and last terms, ac and bd, you can figure out that pesky middle term (ad + bc) with one more multiplication step rather than two. You don’t need to calculate ad and bc individually:

(ad + bc) = ((a + b) × (c + d)) – ac – bd,

Or with our concrete numbers:

((1 × 4) + (2 × 3)) = ((1 + 2) × (3 + 4)) – 3 – 8 = 10.

Pause to notice the weirdness in the equation above. It suggests that to multiply 12 × 34 quickly, you should add the 1 and the 2 in 12 and the 3 and the 4 in 34. This is hardly a natural thing to do. No wonder it took so long for someone to figure it out. It ends up reducing the workload, however: because we’ve already computed ac and bd, the right-hand side only contains one more multiplication, plus some additions and subtractions.

Returning to 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd), we only need three multiplications rather than four. We compute ac and bd in the straightforward way and then use Karatsuba’s trick to compute (ad + bc) with a single multiplication. Plugging in ac = 3, bd = 8 and (ad + bc) = 10 gives our answer of 408.

We shaved one multiplication off the procedure. If that seems measly, Karatsuba has another insight in store. Say we’re multiplying bigger numbers: 1,234 × 5,678. We split them in half like we did before: a = 12, b = 34, c = 56 and d = 78, and we write the problem as (100a + b) × (100c + d) = 10,000(ac) + 100(ad + bc) + (bd).

We can solve this with three multiplications. Those multiplications, however, now involve two-digit numbers. Luckily, we know a way to multiply two-digit numbers with only three single-digit multiplications each! In total, a problem that would take 16 single-digit multiplications the traditional way now needs only nine. By recursively applying Karatsuba’s trick on large numbers, the savings compound. It splits the input numbers in half, then splits those halves in half, and so on, applying this four-for-three trade all the way down. The algorithm works out to have a running time of roughly O(n1.585), which is drastically faster than O(n2). For reference, multiplying a pair of thousand-digit numbers involves a million single-digit multiplications using the grade-school method but fewer than 57,000 using Karatsuba’s algorithm.

The 23-year-old’s efficiency is baked into software that runs every day. Because of its extra overhead (the additions, managing the repeated splitting and recombining of numbers, and so on) its advantages over the grade-school algorithm don’t kick in until numbers grow relatively big. Python, for example, is a popular programming language that is famously smooth at handling integers of any size. If you peek into Python's underlying source code (search “Karatsuba” here), you will see it relies on a hybrid approach. For modestly sized inputs, it uses the grade-school math, but once numbers reach around 630 decimal digits, it flips a switch and employs Karatsuba’s algorithm. That many digits might seem gargantuan by terrestrial standards, but computers deal with much bigger. (Technical aside: On most modern machines Python stores large numbers in base 230, so the linked Karatsuba cutoff of 70 digits in base 230 translates to roughly 630 decimal digits).

Karatsuba’s algorithm ignited a decades-long race to find the ultimate speed limit of multiplication. That pursuit culminated in 2019, when mathematicians David Harvey and Joris van der Hoeven described an extremely sophisticated algorithm that beat Karatsuba’s by more than any of the previous breakthroughs. The new algorithm runs in O(n × log n) time. Here log denotes the logarithm of n, which is a function that grows very slowly. It’s a staggering result. The function n × log n is just a little bit bigger than n itself. This means that calculating the product of two massive numbers requires only a little more time than adding them, or even reading them, would in the first place (it takes n computational steps to read all n digits of a number).

The triumph comes with a crucial caveat, though. Just as Karatsuba’s algorithm only outperforms the grade-school approach when numbers get reasonably large, the Harvey-van der Hoeven algorithm doesn’t pull ahead until the numbers become truly galactic. In computer science, a “galactic algorithm” is a formal term for a method that is impressively efficient on sufficiently large numbers but will never be useful in practice because the numbers are so large.

Even with that asterisk, it was a watershed achievement. It secured the record for the fastest known multiplication method in principle and could pave the way for algorithms that run in O(n × log n) steps, not just in principle, but in practice. Today, theoretical computer scientists widely suspect that O(n × log n) is the fastest possible speed for multiplication, and formally proving it has become the holy grail for this niche field of mathematics. But as history reminds us, widespread consensus is not a mathematical proof. Conjectures about the speed limit of multiplication have been overturned before.

sentences.en: Elementary school students might memorize their times tables for single-digit numbers, but memorization won’t cut it when the teacher asks for three-digit multiplication.
sentences.zh: 小学生可能会记住个位数的乘法表，但当老师要求三位数乘法时，记住并不能解决问题。

sentences.en: This requires an algorithm: students are taught to stack one number atop another and multiply each digit of the bottom number by each digit of the top one.
sentences.zh: 这需要一种算法：教学生将一个数字堆叠在另一个数字之上，并将底部数字的每个数字乘以顶部数字的每个数字。

sentences.en: For millennia, mathematicians believed this to be the fastest multiplication method, until a 23-year-old made a shocking discovery in 1960, which led to a mystery that remains unsolved to this day.
sentences.zh: 几千年来，数学家们一直相信这是最快的乘法方法，直到 1960 年一位 23 岁的年轻人有了一个令人震惊的发现，这导致了一个至今仍未解开的谜团。

At this scale, even a simple operation becomes a bottleneck, and any extra efficiency has global economic consequences.

sentences.zh: 这个谜团对于任何参与数字世界的人来说都是至关重要的，因为乘法是计算机的基本运算。

sentences.en: Encryption, robotics, artificial intelligence, audio processing and pretty much everything else we task silicon chips with involves multiplication, sometimes of huge numbers many times over.
sentences.zh: 加密、机器人、人工智能、音频处理以及我们在硅芯片上处理的几乎所有其他任务都涉及乘法，有时是大量的乘法。

sentences.en: At this scale, even a simple operation becomes a bottleneck, and any extra efficiency has global economic consequences.
sentences.zh: 在这种规模下，即使是简单的操作也会成为瓶颈，任何额外的效率都会对全球经济产生影响。

sentences.zh: 要了解该瓶颈的本质，请观察小学算法如何处理增长。

sentences.en: When you multiply two two-digit numbers, you perform four single-digit multiplications.
sentences.zh: 当您将两个两位数相乘时，您将执行四次个位数乘法。

sentences.en: If you bump that up to a three-digit pair of numbers, you do nine single-digit multiplications.
sentences.zh: 如果将其增加到一对三位数，则需要进行九次个位数乘法。

sentences.en: The workload scales with the square of the number of digits (n2, where n is the number of digits in the numbers being multiplied).
sentences.zh: 工作负载与位数的平方成正比（n2，其中 n 是相乘的数字的位数）。

sentences.en: When analyzing an algorithm like this, computer scientists don’t measure speed in seconds, because that depends on the hardware.
sentences.zh: 在分析这样的算法时，计算机科学家不会以秒为单位测量速度，因为这取决于硬件。

sentences.en: Instead they count the computational steps.
sentences.zh: 相反，他们计算计算步骤。

sentences.en: They also ignore minor bookkeeping details, such as the time it takes to carry a one when multiplying.
sentences.zh: 他们还忽略了一些小的簿记细节，例如乘法时携带一个所需的时间。

sentences.en: When numbers get large enough, those lower-level operations cease to matter, entirely eclipsed by the more intensive operations involved.
sentences.zh: 当数量足够多时，那些较低级别的操作就不再重要，完全被所涉及的更密集的操作所掩盖。

sentences.en: Computer scientists denote the number of steps using what’s called Big O notation: the grade-school algorithm, for example, takes O(n2) steps, which is read as “order n squared.”
sentences.zh: 计算机科学家使用所谓的 Big O 表示法来表示步骤数：例如，小学算法需要 O(n2) 个步骤，读作“n 次方”。

sentences.en: Broadly speaking, if the numbers are twice as long, the algorithm takes four times as much computational work to execute.
sentences.zh: 一般来说，如果数字的长度是原来的两倍，则算法执行所需的计算工作量是原来的四倍。

sentences.en: If the numbers are a thousand times as long, it takes a million (1,000 squared) times as much work.
sentences.zh: 如果数字长一千倍，则需要一百万（1,000 平方）倍的工作量。

sentences.zh: 自古以来，数学家就怀疑 O(n2) 是乘法的固有速度限制。

sentences.en: The celebrated Soviet math professor Andrey Kolmogorov posed the O(n^2) speed limit as a formal conjecture and mentioned it during a 1960 seminar at Moscow State University.

sentences.en: Whenever mathematicians propose a conjecture, they are planting a flag of sorts and waiting for others to either prove or disprove them.
sentences.zh: 每当数学家提出一个猜想时，他们都会竖起一面旗帜，等待其他人证明或反驳他们。

sentences.en: It took just a week for Anatoly Karatsuba, then a 23-year-old student in the audience, to return and prove Kolmogorov wrong.

sentences.en: Kolmogorov was stunned.
sentences.zh: 科尔莫哥洛夫惊呆了。

sentences.en: The result was published in the prestigious Proceedings of the USSR Academy of Sciences, but amusingly, Karatsuba didn’t write it.
sentences.zh: 结果发表在著名的《苏联科学院院刊》上，但有趣的是，这并不是唐叶写的。

sentences.en: Kolmogorov wrote the formal proof himself and submitted it for publication with Karatsuba listed as the lead author.
sentences.zh: 科尔莫哥洛夫亲自撰写了正式证明，并将其提交出版，唐叶列为主要作者。

sentences.en: Karatsuba only found out about the paper when he received the reprints in the mail.
sentences.zh: 唐叶是在收到邮件中的重印本后才知道这篇论文的。

sentences.zh: 将两个 n 位数字相加只需要 O(n) 时间，因为它需要对数字进行一次扫描，而不是像乘法那样对底部数字的每个数字对顶部数字进行完整扫描。

sentences.zh: 为了了解唐叶如何将乘法换成加法，让我们看一个小例子。

sentences.en: The method would be overly complicated for such a simple problem, but it saves a meaningful amount of time when numbers get larger.
sentences.zh: 对于这样一个简单的问题，该方法过于复杂，但当数字变大时，它可以节省大量时间。

blocks.html_en: In this simple example, let’s calculate 12 × 34.

sentences.en: In this simple example, let’s calculate 12 × 34.
sentences.zh: 在这个简单的例子中，我们来计算 12 × 34。

blocks.html_en: First, we split both numbers into their tens and ones digits. Assign a = 1 and b = 2 (for 12), and c = 3 and d = 4 (for 34). Algebraically, we can rewrite 12 × 34 as (10a + b) × (10c + d).

sentences.en: First, we split both numbers into their tens and ones digits.
sentences.zh: 首先，我们将两个数字分成十位和个位。

sentences.en: Assign a = 1 and b = 2 (for 12), and c = 3 and d = 4 (for 34).
sentences.zh: 指定 a = 1 和 b = 2（对于 12），以及 c = 3 和 d = 4（对于 34）。

sentences.en: Algebraically, we can rewrite 12 × 34 as (10a + b) × (10c + d).
sentences.zh: 在代数上，我们可以将 12 × 34 重写为 (10a + b) × (10c + d)。

blocks.html_en: Expanding this gives 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd).

sentences.en: Expanding this gives 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd).
sentences.zh: 将其展开得到 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd)。

blocks.html_en: To solve the equation in the traditional way, one must perform four distinct multiplications: ac = 3, ad = 4, bc = 6 and bd = 8, which is exactly what the grade-school stacking method entails. (Note that we don’t count the multiplications by 100 or by 10 because those only involve plopping zeroes at the ends of numbers.) Karatsuba noticed a brilliant algebraic trick. Once you compute the first and last terms, ac and bd, you can figure out that pesky middle term (ad + bc) with one more multiplication step rather than two. You don’t need to calculate ad and bc individually:

sentences.en: To solve the equation in the traditional way, one must perform four distinct multiplications: ac = 3, ad = 4, bc = 6 and bd = 8, which is exactly what the grade-school stacking method entails.
sentences.zh: 要以传统方式求解方程，必须执行四次不同的乘法：ac = 3、ad = 4、bc = 6 和 bd = 8，这正是小学堆叠方法所需要的。

sentences.en: (Note that we don’t count the multiplications by 100 or by 10 because those only involve plopping zeroes at the ends of numbers.)
sentences.zh: （请注意，我们不计算 100 或 10 的乘法，因为它们只涉及在数字末尾添加零。）

sentences.en: Karatsuba noticed a brilliant algebraic trick.
sentences.zh: 唐叶注意到了一个绝妙的代数技巧。

sentences.en: Once you compute the first and last terms, ac and bd, you can figure out that pesky middle term (ad + bc) with one more multiplication step rather than two.
sentences.zh: 一旦计算出第一项和最后一项 ac 和 bd，您就可以通过多一个乘法步骤而不是两个乘法步骤来计算出那个讨厌的中间项 (ad + bc)。

sentences.en: You don’t need to calculate ad and bc individually:
sentences.zh: 您不需要单独计算 ad 和 bc：

blocks.html_en: (ad + bc) = ((a + b) × (c + d)) – ac – bd,

sentences.en: (ad + bc) = ((a + b) × (c + d)) – ac – bd,

blocks.html_en: Or with our concrete numbers:

sentences.en: Or with our concrete numbers:
sentences.zh: 或者用我们的具体数字：

blocks.html_en: Pause to notice the weirdness in the equation above. It suggests that to multiply 12 × 34 quickly, you should add the 1 and the 2 in 12 and the 3 and the 4 in 34. This is hardly a natural thing to do. No wonder it took so long for someone to figure it out. It ends up reducing the workload, however: because we’ve already computed ac and bd, the right-hand side only contains one more multiplication, plus some additions and subtractions.

sentences.en: Pause to notice the weirdness in the equation above.
sentences.zh: 停下来注意上面等式中的奇怪之处。

sentences.en: It suggests that to multiply 12 × 34 quickly, you should add the 1 and the 2 in 12 and the 3 and the 4 in 34.
sentences.zh: 它建议要快速乘以 12 × 34，您应该将 12 中的 1 和 2 加起来，以及 34 中的 3 和 4 相加。

sentences.en: This is hardly a natural thing to do.
sentences.zh: 这并不是一件自然而然的事情。

sentences.en: No wonder it took so long for someone to figure it out.
sentences.zh: 难怪花了这么长时间才有人弄清楚。

sentences.en: It ends up reducing the workload, however: because we’ve already computed ac and bd, the right-hand side only contains one more multiplication, plus some additions and subtractions.
sentences.zh: 然而，它最终减少了工作量：因为我们已经计算了 ac 和 bd，所以右侧只包含一个乘法，再加上一些加法和减法。

blocks.html_en: Returning to 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd), we only need three multiplications rather than four. We compute ac and bd in the straightforward way and then use Karatsuba’s trick to compute (ad + bc) with a single multiplication. Plugging in ac = 3, bd = 8 and (ad + bc) = 10 gives our answer of 408.

sentences.en: Returning to 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd), we only need three multiplications rather than four.
sentences.zh: 回到 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd)，我们只需要三次乘法而不是四次。

sentences.en: We compute ac and bd in the straightforward way and then use Karatsuba’s trick to compute (ad + bc) with a single multiplication.
sentences.zh: 我们以简单的方式计算 ac 和 bd，然后使用 Karatsuba 的技巧通过一次乘法来计算 (ad + bc)。

sentences.en: Plugging in ac = 3, bd = 8 and (ad + bc) = 10 gives our answer of 408.
sentences.zh: 代入 ac = 3、bd = 8 和 (ad + bc) = 10，得到答案 408。

blocks.html_en: We shaved one multiplication off the procedure. If that seems measly, Karatsuba has another insight in store. Say we’re multiplying bigger numbers: 1,234 × 5,678. We split them in half like we did before: a = 12, b = 34, c = 56 and d = 78, and we write the problem as (100a + b) × (100c + d) = 10,000(ac) + 100(ad + bc) + (bd).

sentences.en: We shaved one multiplication off the procedure.
sentences.zh: 我们从这个过程中删除了一次乘法。

sentences.en: If that seems measly, Karatsuba has another insight in store.
sentences.zh: 如果这看起来微不足道，那么唐叶还有另一种见解。

sentences.en: Say we’re multiplying bigger numbers: 1,234 × 5,678.
sentences.zh: 假设我们要乘以更大的数字：1,234 × 5,678。

sentences.en: We split them in half like we did before: a = 12, b = 34, c = 56 and d = 78, and we write the problem as (100a + b) × (100c + d) = 10,000(ac) + 100(ad + bc) + (bd).
sentences.zh: 我们像之前一样将它们分成两半：a = 12、b = 34、c = 56 和 d = 78，并将问题写为 (100a + b) × (100c + d) = 10,000(ac) + 100(ad + bc) + (bd)。

blocks.html_en: We can solve this with three multiplications. Those multiplications, however, now involve two-digit numbers. Luckily, we know a way to multiply two-digit numbers with only three single-digit multiplications each! In total, a problem that would take 16 single-digit multiplications the traditional way now needs only nine. By recursively applying Karatsuba’s trick on large numbers, the savings compound. It splits the input numbers in half, then splits those halves in half, and so on, applying this four-for-three trade all the way down. The algorithm works out to have a running time of roughly O(n1.585), which is drastically faster than O(n2). For reference, multiplying a pair of thousand-digit numbers involves a million single-digit multiplications using the grade-school method but fewer than 57,000 using Karatsuba’s algorithm.

sentences.en: We can solve this with three multiplications.
sentences.zh: 我们可以用三个乘法来解决这个问题。

sentences.en: Those multiplications, however, now involve two-digit numbers.
sentences.zh: 然而，这些乘法现在涉及两位数。

sentences.en: Luckily, we know a way to multiply two-digit numbers with only three single-digit multiplications each!
sentences.zh: 幸运的是，我们知道一种只需要三个个位数乘法即可将两位数相乘的方法！

sentences.en: In total, a problem that would take 16 single-digit multiplications the traditional way now needs only nine.
sentences.zh: 总的来说，传统方法需要 16 次个位数乘法的问题现在只需要 9 次。

sentences.en: By recursively applying Karatsuba’s trick on large numbers, the savings compound.
sentences.zh: 通过对大数递归地应用唐叶的技巧，节省的成本会增加。

sentences.en: It splits the input numbers in half, then splits those halves in half, and so on, applying this four-for-three trade all the way down.
sentences.zh: 它将输入数字分成两半，然后将这些两半分成两半，依此类推，一直向下应用这种四对三的交易。

sentences.en: The algorithm works out to have a running time of roughly O(n1.585), which is drastically faster than O(n2).
sentences.zh: 该算法的运行时间约为 O(n1.585)，比 O(n2) 快得多。

sentences.en: For reference, multiplying a pair of thousand-digit numbers involves a million single-digit multiplications using the grade-school method but fewer than 57,000 using Karatsuba’s algorithm.
sentences.zh: 作为参考，使用小学方法计算一对千位数字的乘法涉及一百万次个位数乘法，但使用Karatsuba 算法则需要不到 57,000 次。

blocks.html_en: The 23-year-old’s efficiency is baked into software that runs every day. Because of its extra overhead (the additions, managing the repeated splitting and recombining of numbers, and so on) its advantages over the grade-school algorithm don’t kick in until numbers grow relatively big. Python, for example, is a popular programming language that is famously smooth at handling integers of any size. If you peek into Python's underlying source code (search “Karatsuba” here), you will see it relies on a hybrid approach. For modestly sized inputs, it uses the grade-school math, but once numbers reach around 630 decimal digits, it flips a switch and employs Karatsuba’s algorithm. That many digits might seem gargantuan by terrestrial standards, but computers deal with much bigger. (Technical aside: On most modern machines Python stores large numbers in base 230, so the linked Karatsuba cutoff of 70 digits in base 230 translates to roughly 630 decimal digits).

sentences.en: The 23-year-old’s efficiency is baked into software that runs every day.
sentences.zh: 这位 23 岁的人的效率已融入到每天运行的软件中。

sentences.en: Because of its extra overhead (the additions, managing the repeated splitting and recombining of numbers, and so on) its advantages over the grade-school algorithm don’t kick in until numbers grow relatively big.
sentences.zh: 由于其额外的开销（加法、管理数字的重复拆分和重组等），它相对于小学算法的优势只有在数字变得相对较大时才会显现出来。

sentences.en: Python, for example, is a popular programming language that is famously smooth at handling integers of any size.
sentences.zh: 例如，Python 是一种流行的编程语言，它在处理任何大小的整数方面都非常流畅。

sentences.en: If you peek into Python's underlying source code (search “Karatsuba” here), you will see it relies on a hybrid approach.
sentences.zh: 如果您查看 Python 的底层源代码（在此处搜索“Karatsuba”），您会发现它依赖于混合方法。

sentences.en: For modestly sized inputs, it uses the grade-school math, but once numbers reach around 630 decimal digits, it flips a switch and employs Karatsuba’s algorithm.
sentences.zh: 对于中等大小的输入，它使用小学数学，但一旦数字达到大约 630 位十进制数字，它就会翻转开关并采用 Karatsuba 的算法。

sentences.en: That many digits might seem gargantuan by terrestrial standards, but computers deal with much bigger.
sentences.zh: 以地球标准来看，这么多的数字似乎是巨大的，但计算机处理的数字要大得多。

sentences.en: (Technical aside: On most modern machines Python stores large numbers in base 230, so the linked Karatsuba cutoff of 70 digits in base 230 translates to roughly 630 decimal digits).
sentences.zh: （技术旁白：在大多数现代机器上，Python 以 230 为基数存储大量数字，因此以 230 为基数的 70 位数字的链接 Karatsuba 截止值转换为大约 630 个十进制数字）。

blocks.html_en: Karatsuba’s algorithm ignited a decades-long race to find the ultimate speed limit of multiplication. That pursuit culminated in 2019, when mathematicians David Harvey and Joris van der Hoeven described an extremely sophisticated algorithm that beat Karatsuba’s by more than any of the previous breakthroughs. The new algorithm runs in O(n × log n) time. Here log denotes the logarithm of n, which is a function that grows very slowly. It’s a staggering result. The function n × log n is just a little bit bigger than n itself. This means that calculating the product of two massive numbers requires only a little more time than adding them, or even reading them, would in the first place (it takes n computational steps to read all n digits of a number).

sentences.en: Karatsuba’s algorithm ignited a decades-long race to find the ultimate speed limit of multiplication.
sentences.zh: 唐叶的算法引发了一场长达数十年的寻找乘法极限速度的竞赛。

sentences.en: That pursuit culminated in 2019, when mathematicians David Harvey and Joris van der Hoeven described an extremely sophisticated algorithm that beat Karatsuba’s by more than any of the previous breakthroughs.
sentences.zh: 这种追求在 2019 年达到顶峰，当时数学家 David Harvey 和 Joris van der Hoeven 描述了一种极其复杂的算法，该算法比之前的任何突破都击败了 Karatsuba 的算法。

sentences.en: The new algorithm runs in O(n × log n) time.
sentences.zh: 新算法的运行时间为 O(n × log n)。

sentences.en: Here log denotes the logarithm of n, which is a function that grows very slowly.
sentences.zh: 这里log表示n的对数，它是一个增长非常缓慢的函数。

sentences.en: It’s a staggering result.
sentences.zh: 这是一个令人震惊的结果。

sentences.en: The function n × log n is just a little bit bigger than n itself.
sentences.zh: 函数 n × log n 只比 n 本身大一点点。

sentences.en: This means that calculating the product of two massive numbers requires only a little more time than adding them, or even reading them, would in the first place (it takes n computational steps to read all n digits of a number).
sentences.zh: 这意味着计算两个大数的乘积仅比将它们相加甚至读取它们所需的时间多一点（读取一个数字的所有 n 位数字需要 n 个计算步骤）。

blocks.html_en: The triumph comes with a crucial caveat, though. Just as Karatsuba’s algorithm only outperforms the grade-school approach when numbers get reasonably large, the Harvey-van der Hoeven algorithm doesn’t pull ahead until the numbers become truly galactic. In computer science, a “galactic algorithm” is a formal term for a method that is impressively efficient on sufficiently large numbers but will never be useful in practice because the numbers are so large.

sentences.en: The triumph comes with a crucial caveat, though.
sentences.zh: 不过，这一胜利也伴随着一个重要的警告。

sentences.en: Just as Karatsuba’s algorithm only outperforms the grade-school approach when numbers get reasonably large, the Harvey-van der Hoeven algorithm doesn’t pull ahead until the numbers become truly galactic.
sentences.zh: 正如Kaatsuba 的算法仅在数字变得相当大时才优于小学方法一样，Harvey-van der Hoeven 算法只有在数字变得真正庞大时才会领先。

sentences.en: In computer science, a “galactic algorithm” is a formal term for a method that is impressively efficient on sufficiently large numbers but will never be useful in practice because the numbers are so large.
sentences.zh: 在计算机科学中，“银河算法”是一种方法的正式术语，它在足够大的数字上非常有效，但在实践中永远不会有用，因为数字太大了。

blocks.html_en: Even with that asterisk, it was a watershed achievement. It secured the record for the fastest known multiplication method in principle and could pave the way for algorithms that run in O(n × log n) steps, not just in principle, but in practice. Today, theoretical computer scientists widely suspect that O(n × log n) is the fastest possible speed for multiplication, and formally proving it has become the holy grail for this niche field of mathematics. But as history reminds us, widespread consensus is not a mathematical proof. Conjectures about the speed limit of multiplication have been overturned before.

sentences.en: Even with that asterisk, it was a watershed achievement.
sentences.zh: 即使有那个星号，这也是一个分水岭性的成就。

sentences.en: It secured the record for the fastest known multiplication method in principle and could pave the way for algorithms that run in O(n × log n) steps, not just in principle, but in practice.
sentences.zh: 它原则上保持了已知最快乘法方法的记录，并且可以为以 O(n × log n) 步骤运行的算法铺平道路，不仅在原则上，而且在实践中。

sentences.en: Today, theoretical computer scientists widely suspect that O(n × log n) is the fastest possible speed for multiplication, and formally proving it has become the holy grail for this niche field of mathematics.
sentences.zh: 如今，理论计算机科学家普遍怀疑 O(n × log n) 是最快的乘法速度，并且正式证明它已成为这一小众数学领域的圣杯。

sentences.en: But as history reminds us, widespread consensus is not a mathematical proof.
sentences.zh: 但历史提醒我们，广泛的共识并不是数学证明。

sentences.en: Conjectures about the speed limit of multiplication have been overturned before.
sentences.zh: 关于乘法速度极限的猜想此前已被推翻。

content_html_zh:
小学生可能会记住个位数的乘法表，但当老师要求三位数乘法时，记住并不能解决问题。 这需要一种算法：教学生将一个数字堆叠在另一个数字之上，并将底部数字的每个数字乘以顶部数字的每个数字。 几千年来，数学家们一直相信这是最快的乘法方法，直到 1960 年一位 23 岁的年轻人有了一个令人震惊的发现，这导致了一个至今仍未解开的谜团。

这个谜团对于任何参与数字世界的人来说都是至关重要的，因为乘法是计算机的基本运算。 加密、机器人、人工智能、音频处理以及我们在硅芯片上处理的几乎所有其他任务都涉及乘法，有时是大量的乘法。 在这种规模下，即使是简单的操作也会成为瓶颈，任何额外的效率都会对全球经济产生影响。

要了解该瓶颈的本质，请观察小学算法如何处理增长。 当您将两个两位数相乘时，您将执行四次个位数乘法。 如果将其增加到一对三位数，则需要进行九次个位数乘法。 工作负载与位数的平方成正比（n2，其中 n 是相乘的数字的位数）。 在分析这样的算法时，计算机科学家不会以秒为单位测量速度，因为这取决于硬件。 相反，他们计算计算步骤。 他们还忽略了一些小的簿记细节，例如乘法时携带一个所需的时间。 当数量足够多时，那些较低级别的操作就不再重要，完全被所涉及的更密集的操作所掩盖。 计算机科学家使用所谓的 Big O 表示法来表示步骤数：例如，小学算法需要 O(n2) 个步骤，读作“n 次方”。 一般来说，如果数字的长度是原来的两倍，则算法执行所需的计算工作量是原来的四倍。 如果数字长一千倍，则需要一百万（1,000 平方）倍的工作量。

关于支持科学新闻

如果您喜欢这篇文章，请考虑通过订阅来支持我们屡获殊荣的新闻报道。 通过购买订阅，您将有助于确保有关塑造当今世界的发现和想法的影响力故事的未来。

自古以来，数学家就怀疑 O(n2) 是乘法的固有速度限制。 著名的苏联数学教授安德烈·柯尔莫哥洛夫 (Andrey Kolmogorov) 将 O(n^2) 速度限制作为一个正式猜想，并在 1960 年莫斯科国立大学的一次研讨会上提到了这一点。 每当数学家提出一个猜想时，他们都会竖起一面旗帜，等待其他人证明或反驳他们。 仅仅一周后，当时观众中 23 岁的学生阿纳托利·卡拉苏巴 (Anatoly Karatsuba) 就回来证明了科尔莫哥洛夫的错误。 科尔莫哥洛夫惊呆了。 结果发表在著名的《苏联科学院院刊》上，但有趣的是，这并不是唐叶写的。 科尔莫哥洛夫亲自撰写了正式证明，并将其提交出版，唐叶列为主要作者。 唐叶是在收到邮件中的重印本后才知道这篇论文的。

唐叶的天才在于意识到你可以用昂贵、耗时的乘法来换取廉价、快速的加法。 将两个 n 位数字相加只需要 O(n) 时间，因为它需要对数字进行一次扫描，而不是像乘法那样对底部数字的每个数字对顶部数字进行完整扫描。 为了了解唐叶如何将乘法换成加法，让我们看一个小例子。 对于这样一个简单的问题，该方法过于复杂，但当数字变大时，它可以节省大量时间。

在这个简单的例子中，我们来计算 12 × 34。

首先，我们将两个数字分成十位和个位。 指定 a = 1 和 b = 2（对于 12），以及 c = 3 和 d = 4（对于 34）。 在代数上，我们可以将 12 × 34 重写为 (10a + b) × (10c + d)。

将其展开得到 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd)。

要以传统方式求解方程，必须执行四次不同的乘法：ac = 3、ad = 4、bc = 6 和 bd = 8，这正是小学堆叠方法所需要的。 （请注意，我们不计算 100 或 10 的乘法，因为它们只涉及在数字末尾添加零。） 唐叶注意到了一个绝妙的代数技巧。 一旦计算出第一项和最后一项 ac 和 bd，您就可以通过多一个乘法步骤而不是两个乘法步骤来计算出那个讨厌的中间项 (ad + bc)。 您不需要单独计算 ad 和 bc：

(ad + bc) = ((a + b) × (c + d)) – ac – bd,

或者用我们的具体数字：

((1 × 4) + (2 × 3)) = ((1 + 2) × (3 + 4)) – 3 – 8 = 10.

停下来注意上面等式中的奇怪之处。 它建议要快速乘以 12 × 34，您应该将 12 中的 1 和 2 加起来，以及 34 中的 3 和 4 相加。 这并不是一件自然而然的事情。 难怪花了这么长时间才有人弄清楚。 然而，它最终减少了工作量：因为我们已经计算了 ac 和 bd，所以右侧只包含一个乘法，再加上一些加法和减法。

回到 100(ac) + 10(ad + bc) + (bd)，我们只需要三次乘法而不是四次。 我们以简单的方式计算 ac 和 bd，然后使用 Karatsuba 的技巧通过一次乘法来计算 (ad + bc)。 代入 ac = 3、bd = 8 和 (ad + bc) = 10，得到答案 408。

我们从这个过程中删除了一次乘法。 如果这看起来微不足道，那么唐叶还有另一种见解。 假设我们要乘以更大的数字：1,234 × 5,678。 我们像之前一样将它们分成两半：a = 12、b = 34、c = 56 和 d = 78，并将问题写为 (100a + b) × (100c + d) = 10,000(ac) + 100(ad + bc) + (bd)。

我们可以用三个乘法来解决这个问题。 然而，这些乘法现在涉及两位数。 幸运的是，我们知道一种只需要三个个位数乘法即可将两位数相乘的方法！ 总的来说，传统方法需要 16 次个位数乘法的问题现在只需要 9 次。 通过对大数递归地应用唐叶的技巧，节省的成本会增加。 它将输入数字分成两半，然后将这些两半分成两半，依此类推，一直向下应用这种四对三的交易。 该算法的运行时间约为 O(n1.585)，比 O(n2) 快得多。 作为参考，使用小学方法计算一对千位数字的乘法涉及一百万次个位数乘法，但使用Karatsuba 算法则需要不到 57,000 次。

这位 23 岁的人的效率已融入到每天运行的软件中。 由于其额外的开销（加法、管理数字的重复拆分和重组等），它相对于小学算法的优势只有在数字变得相对较大时才会显现出来。 例如，Python 是一种流行的编程语言，它在处理任何大小的整数方面都非常流畅。 如果您查看 Python 的底层源代码（在此处搜索“Karatsuba”），您会发现它依赖于混合方法。 对于中等大小的输入，它使用小学数学，但一旦数字达到大约 630 位十进制数字，它就会翻转开关并采用 Karatsuba 的算法。 以地球标准来看，这么多的数字似乎是巨大的，但计算机处理的数字要大得多。 （技术旁白：在大多数现代机器上，Python 以 230 为基数存储大量数字，因此以 230 为基数的 70 位数字的链接 Karatsuba 截止值转换为大约 630 个十进制数字）。

唐叶的算法引发了一场长达数十年的寻找乘法极限速度的竞赛。 这种追求在 2019 年达到顶峰，当时数学家 David Harvey 和 Joris van der Hoeven 描述了一种极其复杂的算法，该算法比之前的任何突破都击败了 Karatsuba 的算法。 新算法的运行时间为 O(n × log n)。 这里log表示n的对数，它是一个增长非常缓慢的函数。 这是一个令人震惊的结果。 函数 n × log n 只比 n 本身大一点点。 这意味着计算两个大数的乘积仅比将它们相加甚至读取它们所需的时间多一点（读取一个数字的所有 n 位数字需要 n 个计算步骤）。

不过，这一胜利也伴随着一个重要的警告。 正如Kaatsuba 的算法仅在数字变得相当大时才优于小学方法一样，Harvey-van der Hoeven 算法只有在数字变得真正庞大时才会领先。 在计算机科学中，“银河算法”是一种方法的正式术语，它在足够大的数字上非常有效，但在实践中永远不会有用，因为数字太大了。

即使有那个星号，这也是一个分水岭性的成就。 它原则上保持了已知最快乘法方法的记录，并且可以为以 O(n × log n) 步骤运行的算法铺平道路，不仅在原则上，而且在实践中。 如今，理论计算机科学家普遍怀疑 O(n × log n) 是最快的乘法速度，并且正式证明它已成为这一小众数学领域的圣杯。 但历史提醒我们，广泛的共识并不是数学证明。 关于乘法速度极限的猜想此前已被推翻。
